task_description:
이전 Colligi 분석을 통해 생성된 JARVIS 기술문서를 바탕으로,
Alliance가 실제 구현을 시작할 수 있는 수준의 구현 명세서를 작성해 주세요.

이번 작업의 목적은 새로운 가능성을 다시 탐색하는 것이 아니라,
이미 정리된 기술 방향을 실제 코드 구현 단계로 연결하는 것입니다.

따라서 이번 문서는 가능성 검토 문서나 전략 문서가 아니라,
실제로 개발을 시작할 때 기준이 되는 구현 명세서에 가까워야 합니다.

제가 원하는 것은,
이 문서를 읽은 뒤 Alliance가
무엇을 먼저 만들고,
어떤 구조로 나누고,
각 모듈이 어떤 책임을 가지며,
어떤 순서로 구현과 검증을 진행해야 하는지를
바로 이해하고 코딩에 착수할 수 있는 수준의 문서입니다.

특히 다음과 같은 성격이 중요합니다.

- 구현 범위가 명확해야 합니다.
- MVP와 이후 단계의 경계가 분명해야 합니다.
- 모듈과 책임이 나뉘어 있어야 합니다.
- 입출력과 흐름이 코드 관점에서 이해 가능해야 합니다.
- 구현 순서와 검증 기준이 실제 개발 가능한 수준이어야 합니다.
- 과도한 가능성 검토보다 구현 결정을 우선해야 합니다.

이 프롬프트는 정답을 강제하기 위한 것이 아니라,
여러 AI가 기존 기술문서를 바탕으로 더 나은 구현 구조를 재해석하고,
빠진 명세를 보완하며,
Alliance가 실제로 구현 가능한 수준까지 구체화하기 위한 출발점입니다.

분석 결과는
설계 문서를 넘어 실제 구현의 기준 문서로 사용할 수 있을 만큼
명확하고 실용적인 구현 명세서이기를 바랍니다.

# Project - JARVIS 프로젝트

**문서 유형**: 기술 아키텍처 문서  
**회사**: Colligi2  
**프로젝트**: JARVIS 프로젝트  
**버전**: 1.0  
**작성일**: 2026-03-16  

---

## 1. 문서 목적 및 의사결정 요약

본 문서는 Company의 Project로 추진하는 `JARVIS 프로젝트`에 대해, MacBook Pro 16 / Apple M1 Max / 64GB 메모리 환경에서 실제로 설계·구현 가능한 로컬 AI 비서의 실행 구조를 정의하는 기술 실행 문서다. 목적은 가능성 검토에 머무르지 않고, 어떤 범위를 먼저 구현해야 하며 무엇을 당장 하지 말아야 하는지까지 포함한 의사결정 기준을 제공하는 데 있다.

핵심 결론은 다음과 같다.

- 이 프로젝트는 `아이언맨형 범용 자율 비서`로 접근하면 실패 확률이 높지만, `로컬 퍼스트 개인 워크스페이스 에이전트`로 재정의하면 충분히 추진 가능하다.
- 성공의 본질은 모델 크기 경쟁이 아니라 `검색 품질`, `오케스트레이션`, `권한 신뢰`, `시스템 공존성`에 있다.
- M1 Max 64GB 환경에서 기본 상주 모델은 `14B급`이 현실적이며, `30B 내외 모델`은 상시가 아니라 심화 추론용 승격 계층으로 다루는 것이 타당하다.
- MVP는 `텍스트 중심`, `선택 폴더 기반`, `읽기 + 인용 + 초안 생성`에 한정해야 한다.
- 음성, 화면 인식, 접근성 기반 UI 자동화, 일반 셸 자율 실행은 초기 가치 검증 이전에 넣지 않는다.
- 이 문서의 승인 기준은 “돌아가는 데모”가 아니라 `5일 연속 실제 업무에서 하루 3회 이상 자발적으로 사용되는가`다.

## 2. 문제 재정의

초기 질문은 “개인 노트북 안에 JARVIS 같은 로컬 AI 비서를 만들 수 있는가”였지만, 실행 관점에서 더 정확한 질문은 다음과 같다.

`개인 노트북의 제약 안에서, 한국어 질의응답·로컬 검색·작업 초안 생성을 안정적으로 수행하고, 사용자의 문서·코드·작업 맥락을 근거와 함께 연결하는 로컬 퍼스트 워크스페이스 에이전트를 구축할 수 있는가.`

이 재정의 아래에서 먼저 코드로 입증해야 할 가정은 세 가지다.

1. 한국어 검색이 실제 개인 문서와 코드 코퍼스에서 충분한 정확도를 내는가
2. 런타임 오케스트레이터가 메모리 압박, 배터리, 발열, 백그라운드 인덱싱을 통제할 수 있는가
3. 최소 수직 슬라이스가 실제 업무에 재사용될 정도의 신뢰와 편의를 주는가

이 프로젝트는 챗봇 개발이 아니라 `개인 지식 기반 운영체계의 초석`을 만드는 작업에 가깝다. 따라서 언어 모델보다 `검색 인프라`, `권한 정책`, `실패 모드`, `운영 정책`이 더 중요하다.

## 3. 최종 제품 정의

JARVIS 프로젝트의 1차 제품 정의는 다음과 같다.

`한국어로 내 문서와 코드의 맥락을 이해하고, 근거를 인용하며, 실제 작업 전 초안을 제시하고, 승인된 범위 안에서만 제한적으로 도와주는 로컬 퍼스트 개인 AI 시스템`

이 정의에 따라 1차 제품은 아래를 포함한다.

- 선택 폴더 인덱싱
- 문서·코드 통합 검색
- 근거 인용형 질의응답
- 작업 초안 생성
- 대화 히스토리 및 작업 맥락 축적
- 승인형 제한 실행

이 정의에 따라 1차 제품은 아래를 제외한다.

- 무제한 파일 접근
- 접근성 권한 기반 범용 UI 조작
- 일반 셸 자율 실행
- 음성 상시 대기
- 화면 전체 문맥 상시 수집
- “알아서 다 해주는” 완전 자율 에이전트

## 4. 하드웨어 현실과 설계 함의

Apple M1 Max 64GB는 로컬 AI 실행에 매우 유리한 장비이지만, 설계 판단은 벤치마크가 아니라 실제 병행 사용 환경을 기준으로 내려야 한다.

### 4.1 하드웨어 특성
- 통합 메모리 64GB
- 메모리 대역폭 약 `200GB/s`
- CPU, GPU, 메모리 풀이 공유되는 Apple Silicon 구조
- 실제 사용 시 IDE, 브라우저, Docker, 디자인 툴, 메신저와 메모리 경쟁 발생

### 4.2 설계 함의
- “모델이 로드된다”와 “업무 중 상시 쓸 만하다”는 다르다.
- 30B 이상 계열을 기본값으로 두면 swap, memory pressure, 앱 전환 지연이 발생할 가능성이 높다.
- SSD swap 증가는 성능 저하뿐 아니라 장기적으로 쓰기량 증가 비용을 만든다.
- 배터리 모드에서는 추론과 인덱싱 정책이 별도로 필요하다.
- 따라서 이 프로젝트의 차별점은 대형 모델 탑재가 아니라 `상황별 제어 정책`이다.

## 5. 아키텍처 원칙

본 프로젝트의 핵심 원칙은 다음과 같다.

- `로컬 퍼스트`: 기본 처리는 로컬에서 수행하고, 외부 의존은 정책적으로 분리한다.
- `검색 우선`: 생성 품질보다 검색 품질을 먼저 확보한다.
- `근거 우선`: 사실성 응답은 반드시 출처를 동반한다.
- `최소 권한`: 기능보다 권한을 늦게 연다.
- `승인 기본`: 상태 변경 작업은 사용자 승인 전 실행하지 않는다.
- `공존성 우선`: 노트북은 AI 전용 장비가 아니므로 업무 앱과 함께 살아야 한다.
- `측정 기반`: 모든 주요 판단은 계측값과 실패 기준을 갖는다.
- `확장 전 검증`: 음성·화면·자동화는 MVP 가치가 입증된 뒤 확장한다.

## 6. 아키텍처 결정 매트릭스

| 구분 | 채택 | 보류 | 실험 | 제외 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 제품 범위 | 로컬 퍼스트 워크스페이스 에이전트 | 완전 자율 개인 비서 | 제한적 원격 폴백 | 범용 OS 조종 에이전트 |
| 기본 인터페이스 | CLI REPL, 이후 메뉴바 UI | 데스크톱 풀앱 | Raycast 확장 | 음성 우선 UI |
| LLM 런타임 | MLX 우선 | 단일 엔진 고정 | llama.cpp 호환 계층 | 서버급 vLLM 전제 |
| 생성 모델 전략 | 14B급 기본 + 상위 모델 승격 | 특정 단일 모델 확정 | Qwen3-14B, EXAONE Deep 7.8B, Kanana-2-30B-A3B 비교 | 30B+ 상시 상주 |
| 검색 구조 | SQLite FTS5 + 벡터DB + 하이브리드 검색 + RRF | 벡터 단독 | cross-encoder reranker | 키워드 단독 |
| 임베딩 | BGE-M3 계열 우선 후보 | 다국어 범용 모델 고정 | 한국어 특화 임베딩 비교 | 임베딩 생략 |
| 한국어 분석 | Kiwi 또는 MeCab-ko 중심 비교 | konlpy 추상 래퍼만 사용 | Nori 계열 서버형 테스트 | 한국어 형태소 처리 미적용 |
| 인덱싱 | 선택 폴더 + FSEvents 증분 인덱싱 | 전체 디스크 인덱싱 | 앱별 커넥터 | 수동 전체 재색인만 |
| 보안 | 인용 의무화, 승인 게이트, 권한 사다리 | Full Disk Access 기본 요구 | 세션 단위 임시 권한 | 무제한 셸 실행 |
| 자동화 | 읽기 전용 + 초안 우선 | Finder/메일 제한 연동 | App Intents, Apple Events | 접근성 기반 범용 UI 자동화 |
| 멀티모달 | 텍스트 우선 | 음성 상시 대기 | OCR 기반 화면 맥락 | 상시 화면 수집 |

## 7. 계층 아키텍처

최종 구조는 이해관계자 설명에 유리한 4계층 프레임을 사용하되, 내부 구현은 세분화한다.

### 7.1 상위 4계층
1. `인터페이스 계층`
   - CLI REPL
   - 메뉴바 UI
   - 승인/거부, 상태 표시, 인용 표시

2. `오케스트레이션 계층`
   - 의도 분류
   - 모델 라우팅
   - 권한 상태 관리
   - 안전 정책 적용
   - 성능 제어

3. `지식 계층`
   - 파일 수집
   - 청킹
   - FTS 인덱스
   - 벡터 인덱스
   - 대화·작업 메모리
   - 최신성 검증

4. `모델 런타임 계층`
   - 생성 모델
   - 임베딩 모델
   - 재순위화 모델
   - 추론 엔진

### 7.2 상세 구성요소
| 계층 | 구성요소 | 역할 |
|---|---|---|
| 인터페이스 | CLI, 메뉴바, 승인 패널 | 질의, 응답, 출처 표시, 실행 승인 |
| 오케스트레이션 | Governor, Planner, ToolRegistry | 모델 승격, 컨텍스트 제한, 툴 호출 통제 |
| 지식 | Parser, Chunker, FTS5, Vector DB, Metadata Store | 검색, 최신성, 파일-심볼-문서 맵 |
| 메모리 | Conversation Store, Task Log | 장기 문맥과 반복 작업 패턴 축적 |
| 런타임 | MLX, 임베딩 엔진, reranker | 생성과 검색 품질 확보 |
| 시스템 브리지 | FileManager, Apple Events, 제한적 App Intents | macOS 연동 |
| 관측성 | Metrics, Tracing, Failure Log | 성능·오류·회귀 추적 |

## 8. 데이터 흐름과 제어 흐름

### 8.1 질의응답 흐름
1. 사용자가 한국어 질의를 입력한다.
2. 오케스트레이터가 질의 유형을 판별한다.
3. Retriever가 FTS와 벡터 검색을 병행 실행한다.
4. Freshness Checker가 stale 결과 여부를 점검한다.
5. Context Assembler가 상위 근거와 대화 맥락을 조합한다.
6. Governor가 현재 메모리·배터리·발열 상태에 맞춰 모델 계층을 결정한다.
7. 생성 모델이 답변 초안을 생성한다.
8. Citation Enforcer가 근거 없는 문장을 감지해 보정한다.
9. 인터페이스가 답변과 파일 경로, 청크 근거, 신뢰도 배지를 표시한다.

### 8.2 승인형 작업 흐름
1. 사용자가 “정리해 줘”, “초안 만들어 줘”, “파일 이동해 줘” 같은 요청을 한다.
2. 시스템은 곧바로 실행하지 않고 `계획`과 `영향 범위`를 먼저 제시한다.
3. 사용자가 승인하면 제한된 툴만 실행한다.
4. 실행 결과는 로그와 되돌림 정보와 함께 저장한다.
5. 승인 없는 쓰기·삭제·외부 전송은 금지한다.

## 9. 핵심 기술 선택과 선정 기준

### 9.1 생성 모델 전략
현재 시점에서 특정 모델을 최종 확정하지 않는다. 대신 아래 후보군을 Phase 0 벤치마크 대상으로 둔다.

- `Qwen3-14B` 계열
- `EXAONE Deep 7.8B` 계열
- `Kanana-2-30B-A3B` 계열 또는 동급 한국어 강점 모델

선정 기준은 다음과 같다.

- 한국어 설명 품질
- 코드 이해 및 수정 지시 이행력
- 인용 기반 답변 일관성
- TTFT와 tokens/sec
- 메모리 점유와 swap 유발 정도
- 장시간 세션 안정성

결정 원칙은 다음과 같다.

- 기본 모델은 `14B급 또는 그 이하에서 실사용 품질이 확보되는 모델`
- 상위 모델은 `명시적 승격 또는 복잡한 추론 상황에서만 사용`
- 30B 내외 모델이 한국어 품질에서 유의미한 우위를 보일 경우에도 기본 상주는 금지
- 모델 교체 비용을 줄이기 위해 런타임 인터페이스를 표준화한다

### 9.2 런타임
- `MLX`를 1순위로 채택한다.
- 이유는 Apple Silicon 최적화, 메모리 효율, 로컬 배포 단순성 때문이다.
- 단, 모델 호환성 이슈가 있으면 `llama.cpp`를 대체 경로로 둔다.
- 따라서 결정은 `MLX 우선, llama.cpp 호환 확보`다.

### 9.3 검색
- `SQLite FTS5 + 벡터DB + 하이브리드 검색 + RRF`를 채택한다.
- 이유는 코드 식별자, 파일명, 에러 메시지, 한국어 자연어 질의가 하나의 검색 방식으로 해결되지 않기 때문이다.
- 벡터 단독은 코드베이스와 식별자 검색에서 약하고, FTS 단독은 의미 유사성 검색에서 약하다.
- reranker는 Phase 1에서 필수로 넣지 않고, 검색 정확도 미달 시 Phase 2에서 추가한다.

### 9.4 한국어 형태소 처리
후보는 다음 두 축으로 본다.

- `Kiwi`
  - 장점: Python 통합이 쉽고 설치 부담이 낮다.
  - 단점: 일부 도메인 토큰 처리에서 사용자 사전 조정 필요.

- `MeCab-ko`
  - 장점: 전통적으로 안정적이고 형태소 분석 품질이 검증됐다.
  - 단점: macOS 설치·배포 복잡도가 높고 개발환경 편차가 생길 수 있다.

초기 방침은 다음과 같다.

- MVP 기본값은 `Kiwi 우선`
- 한국어 검색 정확도가 목표 미달이면 `MeCab-ko` 경로를 활성화
- 코드 심볼, 파일명, 에러 문자열은 형태소 분석과 별도로 `원문 토큰 보존` 인덱스를 유지

## 10. 권한 및 보안 설계

이 프로젝트의 보안은 선언이 아니라 제품 구조여야 한다. macOS TCC는 불편한 장애물이 아니라 사용자가 신뢰를 판단하는 핵심 UX다.

### 10.1 권한 사다리
1. `권한 0 단계`
   - 수동 입력 텍스트만 처리
   - 인덱싱 없음
   - 가치 체험용 데모

2. `선택 폴더 단계`
   - 사용자가 지정한 폴더만 읽기
   - 문서·코드 검색 가능
   - MVP 기본 단계

3. `제한적 쓰기 단계`
   - 초안 파일 생성, 임시 폴더 출력
   - 반드시 승인 필요

4. `고급 자동화 단계`
   - Apple Events 또는 App Intents 기반 제한 연동
   - 별도 설정과 위험 고지 필요

5. `접근성 단계`
   - 범용 UI 자동화
   - MVP 및 초기 상용화 범위에서 제외

### 10.2 안전 정책
- 사실성 응답은 파일 경로와 청크 근거를 표시한다.
- 근거 없는 단정은 신뢰도 경고를 표시한다.
- 삭제, 이동, 실행, 외부 전송은 기본 금지다.
- 승인 토큰 없는 상태 변경 작업은 차단한다.
- 비정상 루프, 반복 실패, 과도한 파일 접근 시 Hard Kill이 즉시 동작해야 한다.

### 10.3 Hard Kill 및 실패 차단
- 모델 응답이 파괴적 명령으로 분류되면 실행 경로 차단
- 연속 오류 임계치 초과 시 툴 호출 정지
- 인덱서가 과도한 CPU 또는 디스크 쓰기를 유발하면 백오프
- macOS 업데이트 후 자동화 회귀 감지 시 자동화 기능 전체 비활성화

## 11. 데이터 생명주기와 최신성 관리

로컬 RAG 시스템에서 가장 위험한 실패는 “그럴듯하지만 오래된 근거를 사용하는 오답”이다. 따라서 인덱싱은 수집보다 `정화와 최신성`이 중요하다.

### 11.1 수집 원칙
- 사용자가 지정한 폴더만 인덱싱
- 파일 유형별 파서 분리
- 문서, Markdown, 코드, 로그, 노트 파일 우선
- 바이너리 대용량 파일은 기본 제외

### 11.2 최신성 정책
- FSEvents 기반 증분 인덱싱
- 파일 수정 시 메타데이터 변경 우선 반영
- 임베딩 재생성은 지연 큐로 처리
- 최근 수정 파일은 검색 점수에 freshness 보정 적용
- 삭제 파일은 tombstone 처리 후 색인 정리

### 11.3 저장소 비용 통제
- 대용량 저장소는 파일 크기와 변경 빈도 기준으로 제외 규칙 적용
- 임베딩 재생성 빈도 상한 설정
- 대화 로그는 전부 영구 보관하지 않고 요약 계층과 원본 계층 분리
- SSD 쓰기량과 인덱스 증가량을 주간 단위로 기록

## 12. 성능·배터리·발열 운영 정책

Gemini 초안이 제기한 에너지 관점은 반드시 포함되어야 한다. 이 프로젝트에서 팬 소음, 배터리 저하, 발열은 단순 성능 문제가 아니라 채택률 문제다.

### 12.1 Governor 정책
Governor는 아래 지표를 지속 관측한다.

- memory pressure
- swap 사용량
- CPU/GPU 사용률
- thermal state
- 전원 연결 여부
- 배터리 잔량
- 인덱싱 큐 길이

### 12.2 모드별 정책
- `전원 연결 + 유휴`
  - 상위 모델 승격 허용
  - 임베딩 재생성 적극 수행

- `전원 연결 + 업무 병행`
  - 기본 14B 유지
  - 인덱싱 저우선순위 실행

- `배터리 모드`
  - 상위 모델 승격 원칙적 금지
  - 인덱싱 일시 중지 또는 축소
  - 긴 컨텍스트 응답 제한

- `고발열/고압박 모드`
  - 컨텍스트 크기 축소
  - 모델 언로드
  - 답변 품질보다 응답성과 시스템 안정성 우선

### 12.3 권장 임계 정책
정확한 수치는 초기 측정 후 보정하되, 출발 정책은 다음과 같다.

- swap이 의미 있게 발생하면 상위 모델 즉시 금지
- thermal state 상승 시 인덱싱 백오프
- 배터리 30% 이하에서는 긴 추론 차단
- 첫 토큰 지연이 기준을 넘으면 컨텍스트와 검색 개수 축소

## 13. 리스크, 영향도, 대응 비용

| 리스크 | 영향도 | 발생 가능성 | 틀렸을 때 비용 | 대응 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 한국어 검색 정확도 미달 | 매우 높음 | 높음 | 아키텍처 재작업 2~4주 | 형태소 처리 교체, reranker 도입, 코퍼스별 튜닝 |
| 30B 상시 전략의 메모리 압박 | 높음 | 높음 | 성능 회귀 및 사용자 이탈 | 14B 기본, 상위 모델 승격 제한 |
| stale index로 인한 오답 | 매우 높음 | 중간 | 신뢰 붕괴, 원인 분석 난이도 큼 | freshness 체크, tombstone, 재색인 정책 |
| 권한 요구 과다 | 높음 | 중간 | 도입 중단, 보안 불신 | 권한 사다리, 선택 폴더 기본 |
| 배터리/발열 불만 | 높음 | 높음 | 장기 사용률 하락 | 배터리 모드 정책, 인덱싱 백오프 |
| macOS 자동화 회귀 | 중간 | 중간 | 기능 중단 1~2주 | 자동화 후순위, 기능 플래그 |
| 모델 품질 기대 미달 | 높음 | 중간 | 모델 재벤치마크 1~2주 | 후보군 비교, 라우팅 또는 폴백 |
| 인덱스 저장소 증가 | 중간 | 중간 | SSD 사용량 증가, 유지비용 상승 | 제외 규칙, 압축, 요약 보관 |

## 14. 실패 기준, 중단 기준, Plan B

시니어 레벨 문서라면 “계속할 이유”뿐 아니라 “멈출 기준”도 있어야 한다.

### 14.1 실패 기준
- 실제 코퍼스 50개 질의에서 Top-5 정확도 80% 미달
- 사실성 답변에서 인용 누락률 5% 초과
- 업무 병행 상태에서 memory pressure가 반복적으로 악화
- 5일 사용 실험에서 자발 사용 기준 미달
- 배터리 모드 불만이 반복적으로 제기됨

### 14.2 중단 기준
- 검색 신뢰 확보 없이 자동화 기능 확장 요구가 우세할 때
- 최소 권한 구조를 유지할 수 없을 때
- 모델 품질 향상을 위해 과도한 외부 서비스 의존이 필요할 때
- 구현 복잡도가 사용자 가치보다 빠르게 커질 때

### 14.3 Plan B
- `14B 품질 미달` 시:
  - 후보 2, 3 재평가
  - 한국어 특화 모델 라우팅 검토
  - 검색 품질 개선 후 재측정

- `FTS+벡터 하이브리드 성과 미달` 시:
  - chunk 전략 조정
  - reranker 도입
  - 코퍼스별 인덱싱 규칙 분리

- `MLX 호환성 이슈` 시:
  - llama.cpp 경로로 전환
  - 상위 계층 인터페이스는 유지

- `자동화 안정성 미달` 시:
  - 실행 기능 축소
  - 초안·추천 모드로 회귀

## 15. 검증 게이트

### 15.1 기술 게이트
1. 검색 게이트
   - 실제 문서/코드 코퍼스 50개 질의에서 Top-5 정확도 80% 이상

2. 응답 게이트
   - 첫 토큰 2초 내
   - 유의미한 부분 응답 2.5초 내 시작

3. 공존성 게이트
   - IDE + 브라우저 + 일반 업무 병행 시 시스템 반응성 유지
   - 반복 swap 상태 금지

4. 최신성 게이트
   - 수정된 파일이 허용 시간 내 검색 결과에 반영

5. 안전 게이트
   - 승인 없는 상태 변경 작업 0건

### 15.2 제품 게이트
- 5일 연속 실제 업무 사용
- 하루 3회 이상 자발 호출
- “외부 서비스 대신 로컬 JARVIS를 먼저 열었다”는 행동 전환 확인
- 인용 기반 답변에 대한 주관 신뢰도 상승

## 16. 실행 로드맵

주간 계획만으로는 부족하므로 Day 0 전제와 역할 가정까지 포함한다.

### 16.1 전제
- 기본 가정: 1인 또는 소수 핵심 인력 프로젝트
- 요구 역량: Python, macOS, 검색/RAG, 로컬 모델 운영
- 우선 구현 환경: 로컬 개발용 Python, SwiftUI 최소 UI, MLX 런타임

### 16.2 Day 0 체크리스트
- 개발 환경 고정
- 모델 실행 방식 표준화
- 테스트용 코퍼스 선정
- 벤치마크 질문셋 50개 작성
- 메트릭 수집 방식 정의
- 권한 정책 문안 초안 작성

### 16.3 4주 실행안
1. `1주차`
   - CLI REPL 구축
   - MLX 기반 후보 모델 로더 연결
   - 성능 계측 내장
   - 검색 없는 단일 대화 루프 완성

2. `2주차`
   - 선택 폴더 인덱싱
   - FTS5 + 벡터DB + RRF 연결
   - 인용형 응답 구현
   - 50개 질의 기준 1차 측정

3. `3주차`
   - 대화 히스토리 인덱싱
   - freshness 체크
   - 승인형 초안 생성
   - 메뉴바 UI 또는 승인 패널 추가

4. `4주차`
   - 5일 실사용 테스트
   - 검색 실패 유형 보정
   - 배터리/발열 정책 보정
   - 다음 단계 진입 여부 결정

### 16.4 단계 종료 조건
- 1주차 종료: 모델 응답과 계측 수집 가능
- 2주차 종료: 인용 포함 검색 응답 가능
- 3주차 종료: 읽기 + 초안 + 승인 흐름 가능
- 4주차 종료: 실사용 기준 충족 여부 판단 가능

## 17. MVP에서 의도적으로 남기는 기술 부채

기술 부채를 숨기지 않고 예산화해야 이후 판단이 선명해진다.

- Phase 1에서는 cross-encoder reranker를 기본 제외
- Phase 1에서는 메뉴바 UI보다 CLI 안정성을 우선
- Phase 1에서는 완전한 장기 메모리 대신 대화 로그 + 요약 계층만 사용
- Phase 1에서는 자동화보다 초안 생성에 집중
- Phase 1에서는 한국어 형태소 처리 1개 경로만 기본값으로 두고, 대체 경로는 목표 미달 시 도입

이 선택은 부족함이 아니라 의도적 범위 통제다.

## 18. 향후 확장 방향

MVP가 검증된 후에만 다음을 고려한다.

- 음성 입출력
- OCR 기반 화면 문맥
- 제한적 앱 연동
- 멀티 디바이스 컨텍스트 동기화
- 보안 정책이 정리된 선택적 원격 추론
- 반복 작업용 개인 워크플로 자동화

확장 판단의 전제는 언제나 동일하다. `검색 신뢰, 권한 신뢰, 공존성`이 깨지지 않아야 한다.

## 19. Colligi2 기반 문서 생성 방식

본 문서는 Colligi2의 집단 AI 문서화 프로세스를 통해 정리되었다. 생성 방식은 단일 모델 요약이 아니라 `다중 AI 충돌-수렴형 검토`다.

적용된 단계는 다음과 같다.

1. 원 요청의 표면 질문을 실행 가능한 문제로 재정의
2. 각 AI가 아키텍처, 성능, 보안, 한국어 처리, 로컬 퍼스트 관점에서 독립 초안 작성
3. 초안 간 충돌 지점을 비교하고, 과장·불일치·누락 항목을 분리
4. 리뷰 단계에서 실행 가능성, 리스크, 검증 게이트, 실패 기준을 중심으로 재평가
5. 최종 단계에서 평균적 절충이 아니라 `근거가 더 강한 결정`과 `남겨야 할 이견`을 유지한 채 문서 통합

따라서 이 문서는 단순 요약본이 아니라, 상충하는 제안 중에서 Project의 실행 가능성을 높이는 선택만 남긴 Company용 의사결정 문서다.

## 20. 최종 권고

Company의 Project로서 JARVIS 프로젝트는 추진 가치가 충분하다. 다만 출발점은 “로컬에서 큰 모델을 돌릴 수 있는가”가 아니라 “내 문서와 코드에 대해 신뢰 가능한 근거를 제시하며, 실제 업무 중에도 방해되지 않는가”여야 한다.

즉시 채택할 권고는 다음과 같다.

- `14B급 기본 모델 + 상위 모델 승격 구조`
- `MLX 우선, llama.cpp 호환 확보`
- `SQLite FTS5 + 벡터DB + 하이브리드 검색`
- `선택 폴더 기반 인덱싱`
- `인용 의무화와 승인형 실행`
- `배터리·발열·메모리 상태를 반영하는 Governor`

즉시 금지할 권고는 다음과 같다.

- Full Disk Access 기본 요구
- 접근성 권한 기반 범용 UI 자동화
- 일반 셸 자율 실행
- 음성/화면 기능 선탑재
- 검색 신뢰 확보 전 자동화 범위 확대

이 프로젝트의 올바른 1차 목표는 다음 한 문장으로 요약된다.

`한국어로 내 코드와 문서를 근거와 함께 설명하고, 필요한 작업은 실행 전에 초안과 영향 범위를 먼저 제시하는 로컬 퍼스트 워크스페이스 에이전트`

이 목표를 충족하면 JARVIS는 확장 가능한 시스템이 된다. 충족하지 못하면 그 어떤 화려한 기능도 장기적으로 채택되지 않는다.

## 21. 문서 정보

- 회사: Company
- 프로젝트: Project
- 문서 제목: JARVIS 프로젝트
- 버전: 1.0
- 대상 환경: MacBook Pro 16 / Apple M1 Max / 64GB 메모리
- 생성 기준 시각: 2026-03-16 Asia/Seoul
- 참여 AI: Claude, Codex, Gemini, Ollama

© 2026 Company | Project

## Colligi2 생성 과정

이 문서는 단일 AI가 한 번에 작성한 결과가 아니라, 여러 AI가 같은 주제를 교차 검증하며 단계적으로 생성한 집단지성 산출물이다.

1. **의도 재구성**: 사용자의 표면 요청 뒤에 있는 실제 목표, 숨은 제약, 검증이 필요한 질문을 재구성했다.
2. **단계 설계**: 여러 AI가 독립적으로 분석 단계를 제안하고, 연구 및 병합 과정을 거쳐 최종 분석 구조를 설계했다.
3. **다단계 토론**: 각 분석 단계에서 AI들이 의견, 평가, 반론을 주고받으며 쟁점과 대안을 정리했다.
4. **창발 통합**: 단계별 결과를 단순 요약하지 않고, 충돌 지점과 새로운 통찰을 집단적으로 합성했다.
5. **문제 재정의**: 원래 질문이 충분히 정확한지 다시 점검하고, 더 본질적인 문제 정의로 보정했다.
6. **문서 집단 작성**: 여러 초안과 리뷰를 교차 검토한 뒤, 최종 문서를 통합 편집했다.

- 참여 AI 수: 4
- 최종 분석 단계 수: 17
- 총 토론 라운드 수: 24
- 중간 실패로 제외된 provider 기록 수: 0

individual_memos.content:
## Stated Intent

The user wants to transform an existing architecture decision document (produced by Colligi2's multi-AI analysis) into a concrete **implementation specification** that "Alliance" (another AI coding agent) can follow to start writing code immediately — with clear module boundaries, responsibilities, I/O contracts, build order, and verification criteria.

## Inferred Goals

1. **Bridge the analysis-to-code gap**: They've invested significant effort in multi-AI architectural analysis but have zero implementation code. The real goal is to stop analyzing and start building.
2. **AI-to-AI handoff protocol**: This is essentially a prompt engineering task — creating a document structured enough that a code-generating AI can work autonomously without ambiguity or re-exploration.
3. **Prevent Alliance from re-debating decisions**: The user explicitly states "not exploring new possibilities." They want decisions locked down so the coding agent doesn't waste cycles second-guessing architecture.
4. **De-risk the first line of code**: After extensive planning, there's likely anxiety about where to actually start. The spec is a psychological and practical bridge.

## Hidden Constraints

1. **No implementation code exists yet** — not even a `pyproject.toml`. Alliance will need to bootstrap from zero, which the spec must account for.
2. **The Colligi2 document is intentionally vague in places** — model selection is deferred to "Phase 0 benchmarks," Korean morphological analyzer is "Kiwi preferred but maybe MeCab-ko." Alliance needs concrete defaults, not decision trees.
3. **Single developer reality** — the 4-week roadmap assumes one person. Alliance (an AI) works differently: it can't run 5-day usage tests or measure battery drain. The spec must separate "code Alliance writes" from "validation the human runs."
4. **Memory budget is tight (15-20GB available)** — per existing memory notes, design target is worst-case, not the 64GB headline number. The spec must embed this constraint into concrete code-level decisions (model loading sequences, process architecture).
5. **The user is Korean-first** — the spec output should respect that the product is Korean-first, but the user is asking *me* to write in English, likely because Alliance (the coding AI) works better with English specs.

## Likely Real Problem

**The user has over-planned and under-built.** There are 15+ analysis documents, zero lines of code, and the project risks death-by-architecture-astronautics. The real problem isn't "write a better spec" — it's "produce a document that makes it psychologically and practically impossible to delay coding any further."

The Colligi2 document, while thorough, is a **decision document, not a build document**. It says *what* to build and *why*, but not:
- What the directory structure looks like
- What the Python package/module hierarchy is
- What the exact function signatures and data types are
- What the first `pytest` test asserts
- What the dependency list (`pyproject.toml`) contains
- How to wire MLX model loading to the REPL in < 50 lines

**What they actually need is not another document — they need a spec that reads like a series of pull requests**, where each PR has: files to create, code structure, tests to pass, and a "done" definition.

## Misframed Questions

1. **"Write an implementation spec"** → What's actually needed is a **build sequence with file-level specificity** (closer to a `CONTRIBUTING.md` + task tickets than a spec document). A traditional "implementation specification" will just produce another planning artifact.

2. **"Alliance should understand modules and responsibilities"** → Alliance (an AI coding agent) doesn't need prose about responsibilities. It needs **concrete interfaces**: Python Protocol classes, dataclass schemas, function signatures, directory paths. Prose-heavy specs lead to AI reinterpretation, which is exactly what the user wants to avoid.

3. **"MVP vs later phases should be clearly separated"** → The Phase 0 benchmark work (model comparison, Korean search accuracy testing) is **prerequisite infrastructure**, not part of the product MVP. The spec needs a "Phase 0: Tooling & Benchmarks" that produces measurement code *before* any product code. The Colligi2 doc blurs this.

4. **"Modules should have clear I/O"** → The deeper need is **testability** — each module must have a way to verify it works in isolation before integration. The spec should define test fixtures and mock boundaries, not just data flow diagrams.

## Validation Questions

Before writing this spec, the following should be confirmed:

1. **What is "Alliance" specifically?** Is it Claude Code, Codex CLI, Cursor, another agent framework? The spec format should match Alliance's optimal input format (e.g., Claude Code works best with CLAUDE.md + task-oriented instructions, not long prose documents).

2. **Should the spec be a single document or a structured set of files?** A `docs/spec/` directory with per-module specs + a master build sequence may be more actionable than one monolithic document.

3. **Is Phase 0 (model benchmarking) in scope for Alliance, or will the user run benchmarks manually?** If Alliance is expected to write benchmark harnesses, that's a different first task than building the CLI REPL.

4. **Has the user already chosen Kiwi over MeCab-ko?** The Colligi2 doc says "Kiwi preferred" but leaves the door open. Alliance needs a single answer, not a fallback tree.

5. **Is the target Python version and dependency management tool decided?** (e.g., Python 3.11+ with `uv`? `poetry`? plain `pip`?) This affects every file Alliance creates.

6. **Does the user want Alliance to implement the Governor (battery/thermal/memory monitoring) in Week 1, or is a stub acceptable?** The Colligi2 doc implies it's critical, but implementing `IOKit` / `psutil` thermal monitoring in Week 1 may delay the core value loop.

---

**Bottom line**: The user doesn't need a better architecture document — they need a **build manifest**: an ordered list of concrete coding tasks with file paths, interfaces, test criteria, and "done when" definitions, structured so an AI coding agent can execute them sequentially without re-analyzing the problem space. The risk is producing yet another planning artifact instead of the thing that finally breaks the planning loop.

## Stated Intent
The user wants a concrete, actionable **Implementation Specification** for the "JARVIS" project. This document should serve as a direct coding instruction for "Alliance" (an AI developer or team) to begin immediate execution, moving away from architectural exploration toward specific module responsibilities, I/O definitions, and implementation phases.

## Inferred Goals
*   **Bridge the "Architecture-to-Code" Gap:** Transform high-level concepts (e.g., "Governor," "Knowledge Layer") into tangible software components (classes, functions, data schemas).
*   **Prevent Scope Creep:** Explicitly define the MVP boundaries so the agent doesn't waste resources on voice or UI automation.
*   **Establish a "Ground Truth" for Validation:** Define exact metrics and test cases that must be passed before moving to the next phase.
*   **Automate Execution:** Enable "Alliance" to work autonomously by providing a deterministic roadmap that prioritizes implementation decisions over further feasibility analysis.

## Hidden Constraints
*   **macOS Ecosystem Deep Integration:** The system relies on Apple Silicon (MLX), macOS security (TCC/Permissions), and file system events (FSEvents). The developer must be proficient in these specific domains.
*   **Shared Memory Management:** On an M1 Max, the 64GB memory is shared between CPU and GPU. A 14B model takes ~8-10GB (quantized), but RAG indexing and IDEs will compete for the same pool. The "Governor" isn't a luxury; it’s a prerequisite for system stability.
*   **Hybrid Data Consistency:** Keeping a Vector DB and SQLite FTS5 in sync with a changing file system is technically difficult. "Stale indexes" are the primary failure mode.
*   **Zero-Trust Execution:** Since this is a "Local JARVIS," any file-writing action must have a literal "Human-in-the-Loop" gate at the code level, not just a UI suggestion.

## Likely Real Problem
The "Alliance" agent needs more than an architecture; it needs a **Project Scaffolding and Interface Contract.** The real risk is that the agent will implement four disconnected layers that don't talk to each other correctly. The "Real Problem" is defining the **internal API** between the Orchestrator, the Knowledge Base, and the Model Runtime *before* a single line of logic is written.

## Misframed Questions
*   **"Which model is best?"** -> The focus should be: *"What is the standard OpenAI-compatible or MLX-standard interface we will use to make models swappable?"*
*   **"How do we make it like JARVIS?"** -> The focus should be: *"How do we build a 'Contextual Grep' that can generate text based on local files?"*
*   **"Should we use Kiwi or MeCab?"** -> The focus should be: *"What is the abstract 'Tokenizer' interface that allows us to swap Korean NLP libraries without rebuilding the indexer?"*

## Validation Questions
1.  **Search Benchmark:** How will we programmatically measure the "Top-5 80% accuracy" requirement? (Do we have a golden dataset of 50 local queries and their expected file-chunk results?)
2.  **Memory Hard-Stop:** What is the specific "Memory Pressure" threshold (e.g., 70% or 80%) where the Governor must force-unload the LLM?
3.  **The "5-Day" Test:** How will we log "spontaneous usage" to prove the MVP's value?
4.  **Schema Definition:** Are the SQLite FTS5 and Vector DB schemas defined? (Without a shared ID/Path mapping, hybrid search will fail).

## Stated Intent
To create a concrete implementation specification document based on Colligi's JARVIS technical documentation, enabling Alliance to immediately start coding with clear scope, module responsibilities, and development sequence.

## Inferred Goals
- **Eliminate ambiguity** in architectural choices (e.g., "MLX prioritized" → "Use MLX for model runtime, with `llama.cpp` as fallback when `mlx` fails")
- **Prevent scope creep** by explicitly defining MVP boundaries (e.g., "No voice UI in Phase 1")
- **Enable immediate action** by providing executable tasks (e.g., "Implement CLI REPL with `qwen3:14b` model loader by Day 1")
- **Force decision closure** on open choices (e.g., "Kiwi for Korean NLP, not MeCab-ko")

## Hidden Constraints
- Alliance lacks bandwidth for *re-weighing* trade-offs (e.g., they need "MLX *or* `llama.cpp`" → "Use MLX *with fallback protocol*")
- Colligi's doc contains *undecided options* (e.g., "Candidate models: Qwen3-14B, EXAONE-7.8B") that block development
- Team will *default to adding features* if MVP boundaries aren't razor-sharp (e.g., "Include voice UI" despite exclusion)
- No tolerance for "theoretical" specs (Alliance needs *what to code today*, not "why we chose MLX")

## Likely Real Problem
**The Colligi document is a decision log, not an implementation guide.** Alliance cannot start coding because:
1. Critical choices remain *unresolved* (e.g., "Kiwi vs MeCab-ko" is listed as a candidate, not a decision)
2. MVP scope is *described* but not *enforced* (e.g., "No voice UI" but no technical guardrails to prevent it)
3. Technical paths are *qualified* ("MLX preferred, but `llama.cpp` if broken") but not *operationalized* (e.g., no error-handling code examples)

## Misframed Questions
- ❌ "Create an implementation spec from Colligi doc" → *This assumes Colligi is complete*
- ❌ "Define module responsibilities" → *Colligi already has them; Alliance needs *how* to code them*
- ✅ **Actual need**: "Convert Colligi's *decisions* into *code-ready tasks* with zero ambiguity."

## Validation Questions
1. Does the spec explicitly state **which model to use for MVP** (e.g., "Qwen3-14B via MLX" instead of "Candidate models: Qwen3-14B")?
2. Does it **prevent MVP scope creep** (e.g., "Voice UI: *Excluded. Code for voice will cause build failure*")?
3. Does it provide **first-file-to-code examples** (e.g., "Create `cli_repl.py` with `handle_query` function signature")?
4. Does it **enforce technical guardrails** (e.g., "Memory pressure > 80% → auto-disable vector search")?
5. Is **every decision from Colligi's matrix (Section 6) converted to an action** (e.g., "FTS5 + vector search → `retriever.py` with hybrid search function")?

## Stated Intent
The user says they want an implementation specification, not another strategy or possibility review, based on the existing JARVIS architecture document.

They want a document Alliance can use to start coding immediately, with:
- clear implementation scope
- explicit MVP vs later phases
- module boundaries and responsibilities
- code-level input/output and flow
- implementation order
- concrete validation criteria

## Inferred Goals
The real objective is not “write a better document.” It is to reduce ambiguity enough that a delivery team can start building without reopening architecture debates.

More specifically, they likely want:
- a handoff document between research/architecture and engineering execution
- forced implementation decisions where the current document still leaves options open
- a build sequence that minimizes wasted effort in a small-team environment
- a spec that can be used for task breakdown, ownership, and acceptance testing
- protection against scope creep into voice, automation, and full autonomy before core retrieval quality is proven

## Hidden Constraints
Several constraints are implied even though they are not stated as formal requirements:
- Small team constraint: the roadmap and language assume 1 to few engineers, so the spec must optimize for execution simplicity over architectural purity.
- Local-first hardware constraint: everything must remain viable on a MacBook Pro M1 Max 64GB under real daily workload, not benchmark-only conditions.
- Trust constraint: citation quality, permission boundaries, and approval gating matter as much as model quality.
- Korean-language constraint: retrieval and response quality in Korean is a first-order risk, so the spec must emphasize search pipeline quality over agent features.
- Decision fatigue constraint: the current document still contains “candidate comparisons” and “benchmark first” language; Alliance likely needs defaults, not open-ended evaluation space.
- Handoff risk: if the next document stays too high-level, engineering will improvise architecture during implementation, causing drift from the intended product.

## Likely Real Problem
The user likely does not just need “an implementation spec.” They need a document that resolves the gap between architecture and executable work packages.

The real problem is that the current technical document is still partly an architecture decision record plus roadmap. It defines direction well, but it does not yet fully answer:
- what exact components get built in MVP
- what interfaces each component exposes
- what data schemas and storage structures are required
- what is mocked, what is real, and what is deferred
- what the first 2 to 4 weeks of implementation should look like at task level
- what counts as done for each subsystem

Without that, Alliance risks spending time rediscovering the design instead of implementing it.

## Misframed Questions
The user frames the task as “write an implementation spec from the existing document,” but the more useful framing is:

- Not “what is the architecture?”
- But “what exact system should engineers build first, with what contracts, in what order, and with what acceptance tests?”

Also, the current source document still asks some questions that are too open for implementation phase:
- which model among several candidates
- which Korean tokenizer among several candidates
- whether reranking is needed later
- how much UI to include in MVP

Those are valid research questions, but if left unresolved in the implementation spec, they become engineering blockers. The implementation spec should convert these into default decisions, fallback conditions, and change triggers.

## Validation Questions
Before drafting the implementation spec, these are the most important questions to confirm:

1. Is the intended output a true engineering handoff spec with concrete APIs, schemas, and task phases, or a product-facing implementation plan?
2. Should the spec lock specific MVP defaults now, for example:
   - runtime: `MLX`
   - tokenizer: `Kiwi`
   - retrieval: `SQLite FTS5 + vector DB + RRF`
   - interface: `CLI first`
3. Does Alliance need this document to support immediate ticket creation by module/workstream?
4. Should the spec include proposed repository structure, service boundaries, and storage schema, or stay one level above codebase structure?
5. Is the goal to optimize for fastest usable MVP in 4 weeks, or for a cleaner foundation that may slow week-1 implementation?
6. Should unresolved model benchmarking be treated as a pre-implementation gate, or should the spec define one default model path and allow replacement later?
7. Does Alliance already have preferred implementation languages/frameworks for each layer, or should the spec choose them?

The document format must match Alliance's optimal input modality.

## Likely Real Problem

**The user has over-planned and under-built.** The Colligi2 document is an excellent *decision record* but it is not a *build document*. It says *what* to build and *why*, but does not answer:

- What the directory structure looks like
- What the Python package/module hierarchy is
- What the exact function signatures and data types are
- What the SQLite and vector DB schemas are
- What the first `pytest` test asserts
- What the dependency list contains
- How to wire MLX model loading to the CLI REPL in the first working session

The gap is not in *decisions* (those are largely made) but in *operationalization*. The Colligi2 document contains open-ended language ("Kiwi preferred," "benchmark first," "candidate models") that, if carried into the implementation spec, will cause Alliance to re-enter analysis mode.

**What they actually need is closer to a build manifest** — an ordered list of concrete coding tasks with file paths, interface definitions, test criteria, and "done when" definitions — than a traditional implementation specification document. The highest-value output reads like a series of pull requests, not another chapter of architecture prose.

---

## Misframed Questions

1. **"Write an implementation spec from the Colligi doc"** → Assumes the Colligi doc is a complete input that just needs reformatting. In reality, the Colligi doc has *unresolved decisions* that must be forcibly closed, and *missing artifacts* (schemas, interfaces, directory structure) that must be created from scratch. The task is authoring + decision-making, not just translation.

2. **"Define module responsibilities"** → The Colligi doc already describes module responsibilities in prose. What Alliance needs is *how to code them*: Python Protocol classes, concrete function signatures, data schemas, and file paths. 4. **"MVP vs later phases should be clearly separated"** → The Phase 0 benchmark work (model comparison, Korean search accuracy testing) is *prerequisite infrastructure*, not product MVP. The spec needs three tiers: Phase 0 (measurement tooling), Phase 1 MVP (product code), and deferred (post-validation). The Colligi doc conflates Phase 0 and Phase 1.

5. **"Should we use Kiwi or MeCab?"** → Should be reframed as: *"What is the abstract Tokenizer interface that allows library swaps without rebuilding the indexer, and what is the default implementation?"* The spec must provide a concrete default *and* a swap mechanism.

2. **Should the spec be one document or a structured file set?** A `docs/spec/` directory with per-module specs + a master build sequence may be more actionable than a monolith.

3. **Is Phase 0 (model benchmarking) in scope for Alliance?** If Alliance writes benchmark harnesses, that's a fundamentally different first task than building the CLI REPL. If the user runs benchmarks manually, the spec should assume a pre-selected default model.

4. **Are development tooling choices already made?** Python version, dependency management (`uv`, `poetry`, `pip`), test framework (`pytest`), linting — these affect every file Alliance creates.

5. **Should the Governor be real or stubbed in Week 1?** Implementing `IOKit`/`psutil` thermal monitoring in Week 1 may delay the core value loop. A stub that returns "nominal" for all system checks could unblock faster iteration.

6. **What are the concrete SQLite FTS5 and Vector DB schemas?** Without a shared document-ID / file-path mapping defined upfront, hybrid search integration will fail at the seams. This is the single most likely integration failure point.

7. **How will "Top-5 80% accuracy" be measured programmatically?** Does a golden dataset of 50 queries with expected file-chunk results exist, or must the spec include its creation as a Phase 0 task?

---

## Tensions Between Interpretations

### Tension 1: Document Depth vs. Document Actionability
**Claude** argues the output should read like "a series of pull requests" with file-level specificity. **Codex** favors a more traditional engineering handoff spec with APIs, schemas, and task phases. **Resolution**: The strongest interpretation is Claude's — Alliance is an AI agent, not a human team lead. File-level specificity with concrete interfaces will produce better downstream code than high-level engineering prose. However, Codex's emphasis on schemas and acceptance criteria is critical and must be embedded within the file-level structure.

### Tension 2: Lock All Decisions vs. Preserve Flexibility
**Ollama** wants every decision forcibly closed ("Kiwi, not MeCab-ko" — period). **Gemini** and **Codex** emphasize abstract interfaces that make choices swappable. **Resolution**: Both are needed simultaneously. The spec should define **one concrete default** for every open choice (so Alliance can code today) *and* a **Protocol/interface boundary** at each swap point (so the human can change the choice later without re-architecting). Default + interface, not default *or* interface.

### Tension 3: Phase 0 as Spec Scope vs. Human-Owned Prerequisite
**Claude** identifies that Phase 0 benchmarking is prerequisite infrastructure that the *human* must complete. **Ollama** implies Alliance should just pick a model and go. **Gemini** treats the Governor as a Phase 0 prerequisite. **Resolution**: The spec should define Phase 0 as *benchmark tooling code that Alliance writes* but *evaluation that the human runs and decides on*. Alliance creates the harness; the human picks the winner. The spec ships with a concrete default model assumption that the human can override after benchmarking.

### Tension 4: Spec as Document vs. Spec as Codebase Artifact
**Claude** suggests the spec might be better as structured files in the repo rather than a single document. **Others** assume a single markdown document. **Resolution**: This depends on what Alliance actually is. If Alliance is Claude Code operating in-repo, structured files (`CLAUDE.md`, per-module specs, a build sequence file) would be optimal. If Alliance consumes a single prompt, a monolithic document is necessary. This is the single most important validation question to resolve first.

### Tension 5: Governor Complexity in Week 1
**Gemini** treats the Governor as a non-negotiable prerequisite for system stability. **Claude** flags it as potentially delaying the core value loop. **Resolution**: Gemini is right about long-term necessity, but Claude is right about sequencing. The spec should define a Governor *interface* in Week 1 with a trivial "always nominal" stub, then implement real system monitoring in Week 3 after the core search-and-respond loop works. This avoids both risks: no architectural gap, no premature complexity.

claude.name: Project Scaffolding & Module Decomposition
claude.focus: Define the exact repository structure, Python package hierarchy, dependency manifest, and module boundaries that Alliance will create. This stage converts prose-level architecture into file-level reality.
key_questions: What is the complete directory tree, including `pyproject.toml`, test layout, and config files?
What are the exact Python Protocol/ABC interfaces at each module boundary (Orchestrator ↔ Knowledge, Knowledge ↔ Runtime, CLI ↔ Orchestrator)?
What are the concrete data schemas (SQLite FTS5 tables, LanceDB collections, conversation store) with column types and indexes?
How should dependency management be handled (uv/poetry, Python version, pinning strategy)?

claude.name: Decision Closure & Default Locking
claude.focus: Forcibly resolve every open choice in the Colligi2 document into a single concrete default, paired with a swap-ready interface. Alliance cannot code against "Kiwi or MeCab-ko" — it needs "Kiwi, behind a `Tokenizer` Protocol."
key_questions: For each open candidate (LLM, tokenizer, embedding model, runtime), what is the locked default and what is the abstract interface that allows future swaps?
What model does Alliance assume as the default before Phase 0 benchmarks complete?
What are the exact PyPI package names, versions, and platform-specific install instructions for every dependency?
Where does "Alliance writes code" end and "human validates and decides" begin for Phase 0 benchmark tasks?

claude.name: Build Sequence & Task Ordering
claude.focus: Define the precise implementation order as a series of discrete coding tasks, each with file paths, inputs/outputs, "done when" criteria, and test assertions. This is the core deliverable that makes the spec actionable.
key_questions: What is the exact sequence of coding tasks for Week 1 (CLI REPL + model loader + metrics), with each task's entry file, expected output, and first pytest assertion?
Which tasks have hard dependencies (must be sequential) vs. which are parallelizable?
What stubs are needed at each stage to unblock downstream work (e.g., Governor stub returning "nominal" in Week 1)?
What is the minimal "vertical slice" that proves the system works end-to-end (query → retrieve → generate → cite)?

claude.name: Data Flow & Integration Contract Specification
claude.focus: Define the exact data types, function signatures, and message formats that flow between layers. This is the highest-risk failure point — layers that individually work but don't interoperate.
key_questions: What are the concrete Python dataclasses/TypedDicts for Query, SearchResult, RetrievedContext, GeneratedResponse, Citation, and ApprovalRequest?
What is the exact flow of a single query from CLI input to cited response, expressed as function call chain with types?
How does the hybrid search pipeline (FTS5 query + vector query → RRF merge → freshness filter → context assembly) work at the function-signature level?
What is the approval gate protocol — how does a state-changing request get serialized, presented, approved, executed, and logged?

claude.name: Challenge: Gaps, Risks & Failure Modes
claude.focus: Stress-test the emerging spec for missing pieces, unrealistic assumptions, integration seams that will break, and scope that will silently expand.
key_questions: Does the spec account for Korean-first risks at the code level (tokenization edge cases, mixed Korean-English queries, code symbol preservation)?
Are there implicit dependencies on macOS APIs (FSEvents, IOKit thermal, TCC) that require specific error handling or graceful degradation?
Does the memory budget (15-20GB available) actually work when MLX loads a 14B model (~10GB) plus embedding model (~2GB) plus SQLite + LanceDB indexes — with IDE and browser running?
Where will Alliance most likely drift into re-analysis or scope creep, and what guardrails prevent it?
Are the verification gates (Top-5 80% accuracy, TTFT < 2s) measurable with code Alliance can write, or do they require human judgment?

claude.name: Synthesis: Unified Implementation Specification Assembly
claude.focus: Integrate all prior stages into a single coherent spec document (or structured file set) optimized for Alliance's consumption. Resolve any contradictions between stages and ensure the spec is complete enough that Alliance can begin coding without asking clarifying questions.
key_questions: Does every module have: a file path, a Protocol interface, a concrete default implementation, a test file, and a "done when" criterion?
Is the build sequence unambiguous — can Alliance execute tasks in order without decision points?
Are Phase 0 (benchmark tooling), Phase 1 MVP (product code), and deferred scope cleanly separated at the task level?
Does the document format match Alliance's optimal input modality (structured markdown with code blocks, concrete file paths, testable assertions)?
Have all tensions from the intent reconstruction been resolved with concrete decisions rather than deferred to Alliance?

gemini.name: Structural Scaffolding & Dependency Baseline
gemini.focus: This stage focuses on the immediate environment setup and the physical organization of the repository to prevent "architectural drift" during initial coding.
key_questions: What specific directory structure (e.g., `src/jarvis/[interface|orchestrator|knowledge|runtime]`) best supports the 4-layer architecture while strictly preventing circular dependencies?

What is the definitive `pyproject.toml` or `environment.yml` that pins specific versions for MLX, SQLite (FTS5), and the Korean NLP library (Kiwi) to ensure environment parity between the human and Alliance?

gemini.name: Interface Contract & Data Schema Specification
gemini.focus: This stage defines the "connective tissue" of the system, formalizing how modules communicate through code rather than prose.
key_questions: What are the exact Python Protocols (interfaces) and Pydantic schemas (data models) required for the `Retriever` to pass context to the `Generator` in a model-agnostic way?
What is the unified database schema that links SQLite FTS5 text tokens, Vector DB embeddings, and macOS file metadata (paths, timestamps) into a single "Source of Truth"?

gemini.name: Algorithmic Logic & Logic-Flow Detailing
gemini.focus: This stage focuses on the "how" of the core technical bets, specifically the Hybrid Search and the Governor's decision logic.
key_questions: What is the specific implementation formula for the Hybrid Search (RRF - Reciprocal Rank Fusion weighting) and the exact chunking strategy for mixed code/markdown/text files?

gemini.name: Sequential Implementation & Test-Driven Roadmap
gemini.focus: This stage translates the 4-week roadmap into a granular backlog of coding tasks, each with a clear "Definition of Done."
key_questions: What are the discrete, sequential coding tasks for "Week 1" that allow Alliance to deliver a working CLI-to-Model loop before the search engine is even built?
What specific `pytest` or `unittest` cases constitute the "Technical Gates" (e.g., 80% Top-5 retrieval accuracy) that Alliance must pass before moving to the next module?

gemini.name: Implementation Challenge: Platform & Resource Constraints
gemini.focus: This stage identifies "showstoppers" where the architecture might clash with the M1 Max hardware or macOS platform restrictions.
key_questions: How will the system handle a "Search Timeout" or "Memory Pressure Kill" during a large-scale indexing job without corrupting the local SQLite/Vector index?

gemini.name: Final Synthesis: The Actionable Alliance Manifest
gemini.focus: The final integration stage where all analysis is distilled into the primary handoff document for the AI agent.
key_questions: How do we convert all remaining "strategic options" from the Colligi document (e.g., "Kiwi vs MeCab") into "Hard-Coded Defaults" for Alliance?
Is the resulting document structured as a "Master Build Sequence" that Alliance can consume as a direct instruction set, eliminating the need for further architectural re-interpretation?

ollama.name: Decision Closure and Forced Default Mapping
ollama.focus: Focus: Eliminate all open choices in the Colligi2 document by forcing concrete defaults with defined swap triggers, converting "candidate comparisons" into actionable implementation paths.*
key_questions: What is the single, unambiguous default for the Korean tokenizer (e.g., `Kiwi`), and what is the exact interface for swapping to `MeCab-ko` without rewriting core logic?

Which model candidate (e.g., `Qwen3-14B`) is the *only* default for Phase 1, and what is the precise condition (e.g., "if `top-5 accuracy < 80%` for 3 consecutive tests") triggering the swap to `Kanana-2-30B-A3B`?

ollama.name: Interface Specification and Contract Definition
ollama.focus: Focus: Replace abstract module responsibilities with executable Python interfaces, schemas, and data contracts to prevent AI misinterpretation.*
key_questions: What are the exact function signatures (e.g., `def retrieve(query: str, max_results: int) -> List[RetrievedChunk]`) and dataclass schemas for the `Retriever` and `CitationEnforcer` modules?
How is the `Governor` interface defined (e.g., `interface SystemMonitor` with `get_memory_pressure() -> float`), and what is the stub implementation for Week 1?

ollama.name: Artifact Generation and File Structure Blueprint
ollama.focus: Focus: Generate all missing *code-level artifacts* (schemas, file paths, dependency lists) that currently don’t exist in the Colligi2 document.*
key_questions: What is the exact SQLite schema for `fts5_index` and `vector_index` tables, including columns like `file_path`, `chunk_id`, and `freshness_score`?
What is the directory structure (e.g., `src/` with `orchestrator/`, `knowledge/`, `model_runtime/`), dependency list (`pyproject.toml`), and first `pytest` test assertion?

ollama.name: Validation Mapping and Testable Metrics
ollama.focus: Focus: Translate abstract "validation gates" (e.g., "Top-5 80% accuracy") into executable test criteria and human-run verification steps.*
key_questions: How is the "Top-5 80% accuracy" metric implemented programmatically (e.g., `test_retrieval_accuracy(query_dataset: List[Query]) -> bool`), and what is the golden dataset of 50 queries?

What are the *human-run* verification steps for "5-day real-world usage" (e.g., "Track user-triggered sessions via `usage.log`") versus *automated* checks (e.g., "Fail build if `memory_pressure > 0.7`")?

ollama.name: Challenge: Decision Gap Audit
ollama.focus: Focus: Systematically identify unresolved decisions and missing artifacts in Colligi2 that would force Alliance into re-analysis.*
key_questions: What open choices remain (e.g., "reranker in Phase 1" or "MeCab-ko fallback") that require *immediate* closure for coding to proceed?
Where does Colligi2 describe *what* to build but omit *where* (file paths) or *how* (function signatures), creating ambiguity for Alliance?

ollama.name: Synthesis: Implementation-Ready Artifact Integration
ollama.focus: Focus: Merge all prior stages into a single, executable artifact (e.g., structured files) that eliminates ambiguity and prevents scope creep.*
key_questions: How is the final artifact structured (e.g., `docs/spec/` with `module_interfaces.md`, `database_schemas.sql`, `build_sequence.md`) to align with Alliance’s AI agent input requirements?
How are excluded features (e.g., voice UI) enforced via *code-level guardrails* (e.g., `if feature == "voice": raise NotImplementedError("Excluded in MVP")`)?

codex.name: [Scope Lock And Decision Closure]
codex.focus: Convert the existing architecture document from a decision record into a build-ready baseline by freezing implementation scope, defaults, and exclusions.
key_questions: Which architecture choices must be treated as final defaults so Alliance can start coding immediately?
What is explicitly out of scope for MVP, Phase 0 tooling, and post-MVP expansion?

codex.name: [Implementation Surface Definition]
codex.focus: Define the concrete build surface in code terms: repository structure, modules, ownership boundaries, interfaces, schemas, and runtime contracts.
key_questions: What package/file structure, module boundaries, and interface contracts are required so the system can be built without reinterpretation?
What exact data models, storage schemas, and I/O contracts must exist between CLI, orchestrator, indexing, retrieval, runtime, and approval flows?

codex.name: [Execution Sequence And Delivery Planning]
codex.focus: Turn the target system into an ordered implementation path that a coding agent can execute incrementally, with minimal deadlock and clear dependencies.
key_questions: What is the correct build order from empty repo to first usable vertical slice?
What are the deliverables, acceptance criteria, and “done” definitions for each implementation phase or PR-sized step?

codex.name: [Validation And Operational Readiness]
codex.focus: Separate what Alliance can implement from what must be measured or judged by a human, and define test gates at both levels.
key_questions: Which behaviors can be verified automatically through unit/integration tests, fixtures, and benchmark harnesses?
Which decisions require human-run evaluation, such as Korean retrieval quality, usability, thermal impact, and 5-day adoption testing?

codex.name: [Challenge]
codex.focus: Stress-test the draft specification for ambiguity, hidden assumptions, integration risk, and scope leakage before it is handed to Alliance.
key_questions: Where would an AI coding agent still be forced to guess, re-analyze, or reopen decisions?
What failure points are most likely at module seams, especially around search quality, indexing freshness, model runtime abstraction, permissions, and approval safety?

codex.name: [Synthesis]
codex.focus: Integrate the outputs of all prior stages into a single implementation specification optimized for direct execution by Alliance.
key_questions: How should the final document be structured so Alliance can move from reading to coding without additional planning?
How do we combine fixed decisions, code contracts, build order, and verification rules into one practical implementation baseline?

To transform the high-level architectural decisions of the JARVIS project into a concrete, executable implementation specification for an AI coding agent (Alliance), I propose the following six analysis stages:

1. **Structural Scaffolding & Dependency Baseline**
This stage focuses on the immediate environment setup and the physical organization of the repository to prevent "architectural drift" during initial coding.
- What specific directory structure (e.g., `src/jarvis/[interface|orchestrator|knowledge|runtime]`) best supports the 4-layer architecture while strictly preventing circular dependencies?
- What is the definitive `pyproject.toml` or `environment.yml` that pins specific versions for MLX, SQLite (FTS5), and the Korean NLP library (Kiwi) to ensure environment parity between the human and Alliance?

2. **Interface Contract & Data Schema Specification**
This stage defines the "connective tissue" of the system, formalizing how modules communicate through code rather than prose.
- What are the exact Python Protocols (interfaces) and Pydantic schemas (data models) required for the `Retriever` to pass context to the `Generator` in a model-agnostic way?
- What is the unified database schema that links SQLite FTS5 text tokens, Vector DB embeddings, and macOS file metadata (paths, timestamps) into a single "Source of Truth"?

3. **Algorithmic Logic & Logic-Flow Detailing**
This stage focuses on the "how" of the core technical bets, specifically the Hybrid Search and the Governor's decision logic.
- What is the specific implementation formula for the Hybrid Search (RRF - Reciprocal Rank Fusion weighting) and the exact chunking strategy for mixed code/markdown/text files?
- How will the `Governor` module translate raw macOS system telemetry (memory pressure, thermal state from `IOKit`) into discrete operational modes (e.g., "Nominal," "Throttled," "Low-Power")?

4. **Sequential Implementation & Test-Driven Roadmap**
This stage translates the 4-week roadmap into a granular backlog of coding tasks, each with a clear "Definition of Done."
- What are the discrete, sequential coding tasks for "Week 1" that allow Alliance to deliver a working CLI-to-Model loop before the search engine is even built?
- What specific `pytest` or `unittest` cases constitute the "Technical Gates" (e.g., 80% Top-5 retrieval accuracy) that Alliance must pass before moving to the next module?

5. **Implementation Challenge: Platform & Resource Constraints**
This stage identifies "showstoppers" where the architecture might clash with the M1 Max hardware or macOS platform restrictions.
- How will the system handle a "Search Timeout" or "Memory Pressure Kill" during a large-scale indexing job without corrupting the local SQLite/Vector index?
- What is the specific error-handling flow for macOS TCC (Transparency, Consent, and Control) permission failures when Alliance attempts to access protected user folders?

6. **Final Synthesis: The Actionable Alliance Manifest**
The final integration stage where all analysis is distilled into the primary handoff document for the AI agent.
- How do we convert all remaining "strategic options" from the Colligi document (e.g., "Kiwi vs MeCab") into "Hard-Coded Defaults" for Alliance?
- Is the resulting document structured as a "Master Build Sequence" that Alliance can consume as a direct instruction set, eliminating the need for further architectural re-interpretation?

- What are the *human-run* verification steps for "5-day real-world usage" (e.g., "Track user-triggered sessions via `usage.log`") versus *automated* checks (e.g., "Fail build if `memory_pressure > 0.7`")?  

5. **Challenge: Decision Gap Audit**  
*Focus: Systematically identify unresolved decisions and missing artifacts in Colligi2 that would force Alliance into re-analysis.*  
- What open choices remain (e.g., "reranker in Phase 1" or "MeCab-ko fallback") that require *immediate* closure for coding to proceed?  
- Where does Colligi2 describe *what* to build but omit *where* (file paths) or *how* (function signatures), creating ambiguity for Alliance?  

6. **Synthesis: Implementation-Ready Artifact Integration**  
*Focus: Merge all prior stages into a single, executable artifact (e.g., structured files) that eliminates ambiguity and prevents scope creep.*  
- How is the final artifact structured (e.g., `docs/spec/` with `module_interfaces.md`, `database_schemas.sql`, `build_sequence.md`) to align with Alliance’s AI agent input requirements?  
- How are excluded features (e.g., voice UI) enforced via *code-level guardrails* (e.g., `if feature == "voice": raise NotImplementedError("Excluded in MVP")`)?

1. [Scope Lock And Decision Closure]  
Convert the existing architecture document from a decision record into a build-ready baseline by freezing implementation scope, defaults, and exclusions.
- Which architecture choices must be treated as final defaults so Alliance can start coding immediately?
- What is explicitly out of scope for MVP, Phase 0 tooling, and post-MVP expansion?

2. [Implementation Surface Definition]  
Define the concrete build surface in code terms: repository structure, modules, ownership boundaries, interfaces, schemas, and runtime contracts.
- What package/file structure, module boundaries, and interface contracts are required so the system can be built without reinterpretation?
- What exact data models, storage schemas, and I/O contracts must exist between CLI, orchestrator, indexing, retrieval, runtime, and approval flows?

3. [Execution Sequence And Delivery Planning]  
Turn the target system into an ordered implementation path that a coding agent can execute incrementally, with minimal deadlock and clear dependencies.
- What is the correct build order from empty repo to first usable vertical slice?
- What are the deliverables, acceptance criteria, and “done” definitions for each implementation phase or PR-sized step?

4. [Validation And Operational Readiness]  
Separate what Alliance can implement from what must be measured or judged by a human, and define test gates at both levels.
- Which behaviors can be verified automatically through unit/integration tests, fixtures, and benchmark harnesses?
- Which decisions require human-run evaluation, such as Korean retrieval quality, usability, thermal impact, and 5-day adoption testing?

5. [Challenge]  
Stress-test the draft specification for ambiguity, hidden assumptions, integration risk, and scope leakage before it is handed to Alliance.
- Where would an AI coding agent still be forced to guess, re-analyze, or reopen decisions?
- What failure points are most likely at module seams, especially around search quality, indexing freshness, model runtime abstraction, permissions, and approval safety?

6. [Synthesis]  
Integrate the outputs of all prior stages into a single implementation specification optimized for direct execution by Alliance.
- How should the final document be structured so Alliance can move from reading to coding without additional planning?
- How do we combine fixed decisions, code contracts, build order, and verification rules into one practical implementation baseline?

- Abstract: We outline the physics opportunities provided by the Electron Ion Collider (EIC). `★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
**Key insight from this research**: The biggest risk for this implementation spec is not any single technical decision — it's the **contradictions across 5+ existing documents**. The Authoritative Decision (Mar 15) says "text-only, CLI, no voice" for Phase 1, while older documents include voice as core scope. An implementation spec that doesn't explicitly resolve these contradictions will cause Alliance to make inconsistent choices. The spec must declare a clear hierarchy: Authoritative Decision > Colligi2 TASK-E93DF600 > earlier documents.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

Based on the comprehensive technical architecture defined in `TASK-E93DF600.md`, here is the **JARVIS Implementation Specification**. This document serves as the authoritative guide for the "Alliance" development team to transition from design to active coding on the M1 Max (64GB) platform.

---

# JARVIS: Local-First Workspace Agent Implementation Specification

## 1. Project Goal & MVP Scope
The primary goal is to deliver a **reliable, citation-backed, local-only RAG system** that assists in professional workflows (Code/Docs) without compromising system performance or battery life.

### MVP (Phase 1) Requirements
- **Indexing:** Targeted folders (Docs/Code) using Hybrid Search (Lexical + Semantic).
- **QA:** Citation-enforced answers based on local data.
- **Governor:** Dynamic resource throttling based on thermal/battery state.
- **Output:** CLI-first interface with "Draft Generation" capabilities.

---

## 2. Core Technology Stack
| Module | Technology Selection | Rationale |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Language** | Python 3.11+ | Ecosystem support for MLX and NLP. |
| **LLM Runtime** | `mlx-lm` (Primary) | M1 Max optimization. Use GGUF/llama.cpp if prefill latency exceeds 5s. |
| **Search Engine** | **SQLite + FTS5 + `sqlite-vec`** | Local-first, zero-latency, ACID compliant. |
| **Korean NLP** | `kiwipiepy` | Superior sentence splitting and modern spacing correction. |
| **Embeddings** | `BGE-M3` (Multi-lingual) | Industry standard for hybrid search & Korean support. |
| **Monitoring** | `psutil` + `pyobjc` | Native macOS thermal/battery access. |

---

## 3. Module Design & Responsibilities

### 3.1. The Governor (Resource Management)
**Responsibility:** Monitors system state and adjusts LLM context length or indexing priority.
- **Logic:**
  - `Thermal State == Serious/Critical`: Kill indexing, reduce LLM context to 2k.
  - `Battery < 30%` & `Unplugged`: Force 7B model instead of 14B; disable indexing.
  - `Memory Pressure > 70%`: Unload LLM when idle for > 5 mins.

### 3.2. Knowledge Engine (Indexing & Retrieval)
**Responsibility:** Maintains the "Workspace Context" via a Hybrid Search pipeline.
- **Workflow:**
  1. **Watchdog:** Use `FSEvents` to detect file changes (excluding `.git`, `node_modules`).
  2. **Preprocessing:** Use `Kiwi` for sentence splitting and cleaning broken whitespace.
  3. **Indexing:** 
     - **FTS5 Table:** Store text for keyword matching (BM25).
     - **Vector Table:** Store `BGE-M3` embeddings.
  4. **Retrieval:** Use **Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)** to merge Lexical and Semantic results.

### 3.3. Orchestrator (Intent & Citation)
**Responsibility:** Validates user intent and enforces "No Citation = No Answer" policy.
- **Citation Enforcer Pattern:** Use Pydantic models to force the LLM to return a list of source IDs/paths alongside the text response.

---

## 4. Implementation Roadmap (14 Days)

### Day 1-3: Foundation & Runtime
- Setup `mlx-lm` with **Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct-4bit** (or equivalent).
- Implement a basic `Governor` module that logs thermal/battery states every 60s.
- **Success Criteria:** A "Hello World" chat that reports current M1 Max thermal state.

### Day 4-7: Knowledge Pipeline
- Implement `SQLite` schema with `FTS5` and `sqlite-vec`.
- Integrate `Kiwi` for Korean document parsing.
- Implement `watchdog` indexing for a single user-specified directory.
- **Success Criteria:** A search query returns relevant file paths from a local 100-file corpus.

### Day 8-11: RAG & Citation
- Implement the retrieval pipeline (Hybrid Search with RRF).
- Build the "Context Assembler" that feeds retrieved chunks into the LLM.
- Implement the "Citation Enforcer" prompt and validator.
- **Success Criteria:** The system answers "What did I work on yesterday?" with clickable file paths.

### Day 12-14: Polish & Safety
- Implement "Draft Mode" (system proposes file edits or summaries, requires `[Y/N]` approval).
- Hard-kill logic for runaway processes.
- CLI UI refinement (Markdown rendering in terminal).

---

## 5. Core Implementation Snippets (Patterns)

### Hybrid Search Retrieval (SQLite)
```python
# Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) Pattern
query = """
WITH fts AS (SELECT rowid, rank_number FROM fts_idx WHERE text MATCH ? LIMIT 20),
     vec AS (SELECT rowid, rank_number FROM vec_idx WHERE embedding MATCH ? LIMIT 20)
SELECT docs.path, (1.0/(60+fts.rank) + 1.0/(60+vec.rank)) as rerank_score
FROM fts FULL JOIN vec ON fts.rowid = vec.rowid
JOIN docs ON docs.id = coalesce(fts.rowid, vec.rowid)
ORDER BY rerank_score DESC LIMIT 5;
"""
```

### System Governor (macOS Thermal)
```python
from Foundation import NSProcessInfo
def get_thermal_strategy():
    state = NSProcessInfo.processInfo().thermalState()
    # 0: Nominal, 1: Fair, 2: Serious, 3: Critical
    if state >= 2: return "LOW_POWER_RESTRICTED"
    return "FULL_PERFORMANCE"
```

---

## 6. Verification Criteria (Gateways)
1. **Accuracy:** Top-5 retrieval accuracy > 80% on the internal 50-question test set.
2. **Performance:** "Time to First Token" (TTFT) < 2.0s on M1 Max.
3. **Safety:** 0 occurrences of file modifications without explicit CLI approval.
4. **Reliability:** System survives a 24-hour "Wait-and-Wake" cycle without memory leaks.

## References
- [MLX Examples](https://github.com/ml-explore/mlx-examples)
- [sqlite-vec Documentation](https://github.com/asg017/sqlite-vec)
- [Kiwi-Python Docs](https://github.com/bab2min/kiwipiepy)

recommendations: Keep the MVP architecture narrow: `CLI REPL -> orchestrator -> hybrid retrieval -> grounded answer with citations`. Do not add broad automation, voice, or screen context until retrieval and citation reliability are proven.

Implement search as two independent rankers from the start: `FTS5` for lexical exactness and a vector index for semantic recall. Fuse with `RRF`, not hand-tuned score mixing, because it is simpler and already standard in production hybrid search systems.
Use separate ingestion pipelines for `documents` and `code`.
Documents: heading-aware chunking, paragraph windows, title/path boosts.
Code: Tree-sitter symbol chunks plus surrounding context, preserve file path, language, symbol name, and line spans.

Store citation metadata as first-class data, not formatted text: `file_path`, `chunk_id`, `line_start`, `line_end`, `mtime`, `content_hash`, and `retrieval_score`. Invalidate or warn on citations whose `mtime/hash` no longer matches source.
Use Kiwi as the default Korean tokenizer for MVP, but design the tokenizer behind an interface so MeCab-ko can be swapped in if Korean retrieval benchmarks miss target.
Preserve dual lexical views for Korean and code: morpheme tokens for Korean text and raw/original tokens for filenames, identifiers, stack traces, package names, and command lines.
Prefer an embedded storage path first.
Recommended MVP: `SQLite FTS5 + SQLite metadata tables + embedded vector store`.
If minimizing moving parts matters most, evaluate `SQLite Vec1`.
If ecosystem maturity matters more, use `LanceDB` or `Qdrant local mode`.
Treat reranking as a feature flag. Ship without it if Top-5 retrieval already clears the gate; add multilingual reranking only if failure analysis shows noisy top-k results.
Build indexing as `initial scan + persistent event stream + background re-embed queue + periodic reconciliation scan`. Do not rely on FSEvents alone for correctness.

Expose governor states explicitly in product logic: `plugged-in idle`, `plugged-in active`, `battery`, `thermal warning`, `memory pressure`. Each state should define allowed model tier, max context, indexing concurrency, and whether re-embedding is paused.

Follow Apple’s minimum-permission path: user-selected folders, persisted via security-scoped bookmarks if needed. Avoid Full Disk Access and keep “write” capabilities behind a separate approval layer.
Add explicit operator controls and observability early: `reindex scope`, `queue length`, `last successful embed`, `stale chunks`, `current model tier`, `thermal state`, and `memory pressure events`.
Make evaluation part of the implementation spec, not a later QA task.
Retrieval: `Recall@5`, `nDCG@5`, lexical-hit rate for filenames/symbols, freshness-hit rate.
Answering: citation presence rate, faithfulness/groundedness, unsupported-claim rate.
Runtime: TTFT, tokens/sec, swap occurrence, background indexing CPU time, battery impact.
Use approval-gated actions only after the system can first produce a readable plan and impacted file list. The plan object should be generated and rendered before any state-changing tool is callable.
references: Apple sandbox file access / user-selected files: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/xcode/configuring-the-macos-app-sandbox/
Apple File System Events Programming Guide: https://developer.apple.com/library/archive/documentation/Darwin/Conceptual/FSEvents_ProgGuide/Introduction/Introduction.html
Apple FSEvents usage and persistent event IDs: https://developer.apple.com/library/archive/documentation/Darwin/Conceptual/FSEvents_ProgGuide/UsingtheFSEventsFramework/UsingtheFSEventsFramework.html
research_results.raw_response:
## Findings
- Hybrid retrieval is the current default pattern for assistant-grade search. Official Azure AI Search guidance runs keyword and vector queries in parallel and fuses them with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF), which aligns well with the JARVIS direction of `FTS5 + vector + RRF`.
- SQLite FTS5 remains a strong lexical core for local search because it gives built-in ranking (`bm25`/`rank`) plus snippet/highlight support, which is useful for citation UX and exact-match retrieval of filenames, symbols, error strings, and Korean raw tokens.
- For a local-first Mac build, an embedded vector layer is preferable to a server-first dependency. Current practical options are `SQLite Vec1` inside SQLite itself, `LanceDB` as an embedded in-process store, or `Qdrant` local mode for a later migration path.
- Code and prose should not share the same chunking strategy. Tree-sitter is the standard parser for code-aware chunking because it supports incremental parsing, syntax trees, and robust parsing even with syntax errors.
- Korean retrieval quality will likely depend on lexical normalization more than on model size. Kiwi is practical for MVP because it is fast, supports user dictionaries, and is easier to integrate than MeCab-ko on macOS; keep raw token indexing alongside morpheme indexing for code symbols and filenames.
- BGE-M3 is a strong fit for multilingual/Korean retrieval because it supports dense, sparse, and multi-vector retrieval in one model and explicitly recommends `hybrid retrieval + reranking`.
- Cross-encoder reranking is a common phase-2 upgrade, not an MVP requirement. BGE reranker docs position rerankers as higher-accuracy filters after retrieval, which supports the staged plan in the attached architecture.
- Retrieval quality is the hard gate for product viability. Multiple sources emphasize that retrieval sets the ceiling for answer quality; in practice, teams measure retrieval separately with `Recall@k`, `Precision@k`, and `nDCG@k`, then measure grounded generation with faithfulness/context metrics.
- macOS file access best practice is user-selected access, not blanket disk access. Apple’s sandbox guidance favors `user-selected` file entitlements plus security-scoped bookmarks for persistence, which matches the “selected folder only” design and argues against requesting Full Disk Access for MVP.
- If the app is sandboxed, security-scoped bookmarks need strict lifecycle handling. Apple explicitly warns to balance `startAccessingSecurityScopedResource()` and `stopAccessingSecurityScopedResource()` to avoid leaking kernel resources.
- FSEvents is the right primitive for incremental indexing on macOS, but it is directory-level, persistent, and imperfect by itself. Apple recommends storing the last event ID and resuming from it; because deleted paths can linger and notifications are coarse, you still need tombstones and periodic reconciliation scans.
- File writes and indexing should be coordinated. Apple’s file coordinator/presenter guidance exists specifically to prevent corruption or races when multiple processes touch the same files.
- A governor for thermals, power, and memory is not optional on Apple laptops. Apple exposes `thermalState`, thermal change notifications, low power mode state, and memory pressure dispatch sources; these are the right OS signals for model downgrades, pausing embedding jobs, and shrinking context windows.
- Industry examples reinforce that retrieval/index efficiency matters as much as model quality. GitHub reported a newer Copilot embedding path improved retrieval quality, doubled throughput, and reduced index memory size substantially, which supports treating retrieval/indexing as first-class engineering work.
- Operational controls for local indexing are necessary. Continue’s docs and troubleshooting show real-world issues around local code indexing, rebuild flows, CPU feature mismatches, and state resets; JARVIS should expose explicit `pause`, `resume`, `rebuild`, `last indexed`, and `scope` controls from day one.
- For automation, Apple’s App Intents are a safer expansion path than generic UI scripting. They expose explicit, typed actions to the system and fit the “approved, limited actions” model better than accessibility-driven general automation.

## Recommendations
- Keep the MVP architecture narrow: `CLI REPL -> orchestrator -> hybrid retrieval -> grounded answer with citations`. Do not add broad automation, voice, or screen context until retrieval and citation reliability are proven.
- Implement search as two independent rankers from the start: `FTS5` for lexical exactness and a vector index for semantic recall. Fuse with `RRF`, not hand-tuned score mixing, because it is simpler and already standard in production hybrid search systems.
- Use separate ingestion pipelines for `documents` and `code`.
  - Documents: heading-aware chunking, paragraph windows, title/path boosts.
  - Code: Tree-sitter symbol chunks plus surrounding context, preserve file path, language, symbol name, and line spans.
- Store citation metadata as first-class data, not formatted text: `file_path`, `chunk_id`, `line_start`, `line_end`, `mtime`, `content_hash`, and `retrieval_score`. Invalidate or warn on citations whose `mtime/hash` no longer matches source.
- Use Kiwi as the default Korean tokenizer for MVP, but design the tokenizer behind an interface so MeCab-ko can be swapped in if Korean retrieval benchmarks miss target.
- Preserve dual lexical views for Korean and code: morpheme tokens for Korean text and raw/original tokens for filenames, identifiers, stack traces, package names, and command lines.
- Prefer an embedded storage path first.
  - Recommended MVP: `SQLite FTS5 + SQLite metadata tables + embedded vector store`.
  - If minimizing moving parts matters most, evaluate `SQLite Vec1`.
  - If ecosystem maturity matters more, use `LanceDB` or `Qdrant local mode`.
- Treat reranking as a feature flag. Ship without it if Top-5 retrieval already clears the gate; add multilingual reranking only if failure analysis shows noisy top-k results.
- Build indexing as `initial scan + persistent event stream + background re-embed queue + periodic reconciliation scan`. Do not rely on FSEvents alone for correctness.
- Expose governor states explicitly in product logic: `plugged-in idle`, `plugged-in active`, `battery`, `thermal warning`, `memory pressure`. Each state should define allowed model tier, max context, indexing concurrency, and whether re-embedding is paused.
- Follow Apple’s minimum-permission path: user-selected folders, persisted via security-scoped bookmarks if needed. Avoid Full Disk Access and keep “write” capabilities behind a separate approval layer.
- Add explicit operator controls and observability early: `reindex scope`, `queue length`, `last successful embed`, `stale chunks`, `current model tier`, `thermal state`, and `memory pressure events`.
- Make evaluation part of the implementation spec, not a later QA task.
  - Retrieval: `Recall@5`, `nDCG@5`, lexical-hit rate for filenames/symbols, freshness-hit rate.
  - Answering: citation presence rate, faithfulness/groundedness, unsupported-claim rate.
  - Runtime: TTFT, tokens/sec, swap occurrence, background indexing CPU time, battery impact.
- Use approval-gated actions only after the system can first produce a readable plan and impacted file list. The plan object should be generated and rendered before any state-changing tool is callable.

## References

- SQLite Vec1: https://sqlite.org/vec1

- Apple File System Events Programming Guide: https://developer.apple.com/library/archive/documentation/Darwin/Conceptual/FSEvents_ProgGuide/Introduction/Introduction.html
- Apple FSEvents usage and persistent event IDs: https://developer.apple.com/library/archive/documentation/Darwin/Conceptual/FSEvents_ProgGuide/UsingtheFSEventsFramework/UsingtheFSEventsFramework.html
- Apple file coordination: https://developer.apple.com/library/archive/documentation/FileManagement/Conceptual/FileSystemProgrammingGuide/FileCoordinators/FileCoordinators.html
- Apple thermal state guidance: https://developer.apple.com/library/archive/documentation/Performance/Conceptual/power_efficiency_guidelines_osx/RespondToThermalStateChanges.html
- Apple memory pressure dispatch events: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/dispatch/dispatchsource/memorypressureevent/critical

- Azure RRF ranking details: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/hybrid-search-ranking
- Tree-sitter overview: https://tree-sitter.github.io/tree-sitter/

- Kiwi tokenizer: https://github.com/bab2min/Kiwi
